hero.title: Rebuilding the coordination layer for a 340-depot fuel distributor
hero.lead: Delivery failures were happening daily. Leadership found out two days later. The problem wasn't execution — it was that the system had no way to surface them earlier.

stats.label: Average issue resolution, down from 48 hrs
Depots on unified platform at launch

problem.title: Decisions were being made on information that was already 48 hours old
problem.description: The distributor operated across 340 depots in three states. Each depot reported delivery status through a regional coordinator, who compiled it into a spreadsheet that was shared with the operations director by end of day. By the time a delivery failure reached the right person, the opportunity to intervene had passed.
problem.additional: The company had tried two software platforms before engaging us. Both failed for the same reason: they automated the reporting process without changing the underlying information architecture. The data still flowed through the same bottlenecks — it just arrived in a different inbox.

content.pullQuote: "The data still flowed through the same bottlenecks — it just arrived in a different inbox."

findings.title: Three structural problems, not one software problem
findings.description: Our diagnosis phase identified three distinct issues. First, there was no real-time delivery status — status updates were event-driven, not continuous. Second, exception escalation was informal — there was no defined threshold at which a late delivery automatically surfaced to operations. Third, depot accountability was ambiguous — the same delivery could be blamed on three different teams without any of them being wrong.
findings.additional: These weren't fixable by changing software. They required a structural redesign of how delivery status moved through the organisation.

solution.title: A unified coordination layer — visible, accountable, and self-escalating
solution.description: We built a real-time operations platform that captured delivery status continuously from driver devices, defined escalation thresholds for each depot tier, and surfaced exceptions to the right person automatically — without requiring a coordinator to notice and forward them.
solution.additional: The platform launched depot by depot over 11 weeks. We designed the rollout sequence to minimise disruption: each depot was onboarded during a low-volume window, with the legacy process running in parallel until the team was confident.

result.title: On-time delivery improved. More importantly, leadership now knows immediately.
result.description: Six months after full deployment, on-time delivery had improved from 84% to 98.3%. The more significant change was structural: the operations director now sees exceptions in real time. The average time from issue occurrence to resolution dropped from 48 hours to 2 hours.

related.title: Multi-location coordination platform for 22-clinic group
related.excerpt: Scheduling, supply ordering, and clinical handoffs across 22 locations were managed through individual WhatsApp groups.

related.title: Fleet operations platform for enterprise logistics provider
related.excerpt: Real-time visibility across 1,200+ vehicles was theoretically possible but practically absent.
